news_summary: Aid workers are overcoming conflict and border challenges to deliver emergency food to hungry Afghan children, while influential voices advocate for thoughtful ethical guidance on AI at the highest global and religious institutions.
news_summary_fr: Les travailleurs humanitaires surmontent les conflits et les obstacles liés aux frontières pour acheminer de l'aide alimentaire d'urgence aux enfants afghans qui souffrent de la faim, tandis que des personnalités influentes plaident en faveur de directives éthiques réfléchies en matière d'intelligence artificielle au sein des plus hautes institutions mondiales et religieuses.
news_summary_es: Los trabajadores humanitarios están superando los conflictos y las dificultades fronterizas para hacer llegar ayuda alimentaria de emergencia a los niños afganos que padecen hambre, mientras que figuras influyentes abogan por unas directrices éticas bien fundamentadas sobre la inteligencia artificial en las instituciones mundiales y religiosas de mayor relevancia.

articles.title: Instability, war and closed borders: How aid workers get emergency food to hungry Afghan children
articles.summary: The odyssey begins in Indonesia and ends, 15,000 kilometres later, in Kabul, the capital of Afghanistan. Along the way, critical food aid destined for young Afghan students crosses nine countries, over land and sea, skirting geopolitical unrest and conflict zones.
articles.content:
Hundreds of thousands of people in Afghanistan face hunger and poverty. The country suffers from repeated floods and earthquakes, declining humanitarian funding and two crises along its borders.

Logjams and logistics

For many Afghan schoolchildren, the fortified biscuits distributed by the World Food Programme (WFP) are often the most nutritious food they will receive all day. But getting the supplies into the country is a logistical minefield.

Take, for example, the 397 metric tons of this key nutritional boost, intended for some 172,000 students, shipped from Indonesia’s Surabaya port, part of a US$3.5 million contribution from the Government of Indonesia to support WFP school meals in Afghanistan.

The supplies are first sent by boat to the southern Pakistani port of Karachi, but from there things get more complicated.

The original plan was for the shipment to be transferred to trucks for a 7,000 km trip through Pakistan but, amid tensions between the country and Afghanistan, the border was closed.

Hunger cannot wait

A new route has to be found quickly because, as Corinne Fleischer, Director of WFP Supply Chain and Delivery, says, “hunger doesn’t wait for routes to reopen”.

WFP shipping officers reroute the cargo to the port of Jebel Ali in Dubai, with a plan to ship it across the Persian Gulf to Iran and then move it on by road.

© WFP/Isheeta Sumra

However, geopolitics strikes again and, as instability spread across the Middle East, in effect closing the critical Strait of Hormuz since March, WFP is forced to rethink the plan once more.

Inside WFP operations rooms, logisticians go back to basics, poring over maps to see whether the region’s geography might offer a solution.

They find one: an entirely new land corridor from Dubai to landlocked Afghanistan across the Caucasus. It’s costlier, more complex and adds another 8,000 km to the journey, but it is the only remaining option.

New route, new hope

One overcast morning, a 21-truck convoy rumbles out of Dubai and heads out along the desert highways of the United Arab Emirates and Saudi Arabia, up through Jordan, Syria, Türkiye and Georgia before boarding a ferry in Baku, Azerbaijan, and crossing the Caspian Sea to Turkmenistan.

Days later, the trucks cross into Afghanistan through the remote Torghundi border crossing, before continuing on to Kabul. Every country the convoy passes through requires new customs clearances, security assessments, transport permits and coordination across seven borders.

Along the route, truck drivers face long waits at border crossings, signing paperwork and snatching moments of sleep beneath open skies.

© WFP/Arete

“I remember the ferry line at Alat port [Baku] where hundreds of trucks were waiting to cross – the line was close to 30 km long,” says Hüseyin Sarraç Ulus, a Turkish truck driver who made the roughly 3,000 km journey from Dubai to the Caspian Sea.

Working day and night

“We drove around 11 hours a day and slept in the truck cabin most nights – it was not always comfortable, but we are used to it," he recalls. "We ate simple food like soup, bread, rice and tea. But it felt good. Knowing the cargo was helping children made me proud to be part of the journey.”

Inside a World Food Programme (WFP) warehouse on the outskirts of Kabul, Abdul Ahad Monib watches as the trucks slowly back into unloading bays.

“There was a feeling of relief when we saw the trucks arrive,” says Mr. Monib, a WFP Supply Chain and Delivery officer. “We followed every step of the journey closely – every delay, every border crossing, every change of plan.

After weeks on the road, the biscuits reach the hands of girls and boys in schools across Ghor, Nuristan and Paktika provinces, in central, northeastern and eastern Afghanistan, respectively.

“For the children, it’s a packet of biscuits that helps them stay healthy,” says Monib. “For us, it’s a logistics feat. No one sees the thousands of kilometres, the delays or the rerouting behind each packet. But that’s exactly the point – whatever the obstacles, WFP delivers.”

This story was first published on the WFP website.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant humanitarian effort by the World Food Programme to deliver critical food aid to hundreds of thousands of hungry Afghan children despite immense logistical and geopolitical challenges. The story highlights the successful overcoming of complex barriers to provide essential nutrition to vulnerable populations, demonstrating a positive real-world impact with broad humanitarian significance and detailed context.
articles.personality_title: World Food Programme finds new route to deliver food aid to Afghan children
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Afghanistan faces hunger and poverty worsened by natural disasters and border conflicts. Many children rely on fortified biscuits from the World Food Programme (WFP) as their main nutritious meal.

**What happened** – A shipment of 397 metric tons of biscuits, meant for 172,000 Afghan students and donated by Indonesia, started its journey from Indonesia to Afghanistan. The original route through Pakistan was blocked due to border closures. WFP quickly found a new path: trucks traveled from Dubai through seven countries, including Jordan, Syria, and Türkiye, crossed the Caspian Sea by ferry, and finally reached Afghanistan after weeks on the road.

**Impact** – This long and complex journey shows how WFP overcame political tensions and closed borders to deliver food where it is desperately needed. The biscuits help children stay healthy in provinces like Ghor, Nuristan, and Paktika. The story highlights the skill and dedication of drivers, logisticians, and staff who ensured the aid reached its destination despite many challenges.

**What's next step** – WFP will continue to find creative solutions to deliver aid amid unstable regions. They will keep monitoring routes and working with countries to maintain the flow of essential supplies to Afghan children.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite political and geographical obstacles, the World Food Programme successfully delivered vital food aid to thousands of hungry Afghan children by finding a new, longer route across multiple countries.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Programme alimentaire mondial trouve une nouvelle route pour livrer de la nourriture aux enfants afghans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Afghanistan souffre de faim et de pauvreté aggravées par des catastrophes naturelles et des conflits aux frontières. Beaucoup d'enfants dépendent des biscuits fortifiés du Programme alimentaire mondial (PAM) comme repas nutritif principal.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une cargaison de 397 tonnes de biscuits, destinée à 172 000 élèves afghans et offerte par l'Indonésie, a commencé son voyage depuis l'Indonésie vers l'Afghanistan. La route prévue par le Pakistan a été bloquée en raison de la fermeture des frontières. Le PAM a rapidement trouvé un nouvel itinéraire : des camions sont partis de Dubaï en traversant sept pays, dont la Jordanie, la Syrie et la Turquie, ont traversé la mer Caspienne en ferry, puis sont arrivés en Afghanistan après plusieurs semaines de route.

**Impact** – Ce long parcours compliqué montre comment le PAM a surmonté des tensions politiques et des frontières fermées pour livrer de la nourriture là où elle est très nécessaire. Les biscuits aident les enfants à rester en bonne santé dans des provinces comme Ghor, Nuristan et Paktika. L'histoire met en avant les compétences et le dévouement des chauffeurs, logisticiens et employés qui ont assuré la livraison malgré de nombreux défis.

**Prochaine étape** – Le PAM continuera à trouver des solutions créatives pour acheminer l'aide dans des régions instables. Il surveillera les routes et travaillera avec les pays pour maintenir l'approvisionnement essentiel aux enfants afghans.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Malgré les obstacles politiques et géographiques, le Programme alimentaire mondial a réussi à livrer une aide alimentaire vitale à des milliers d'enfants afghans affamés en empruntant une nouvelle route plus longue à travers plusieurs pays.
articles.personality_title_es: El Programa Mundial de Alimentos encuentra nueva ruta para entregar ayuda alimentaria a niños afganos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Afganistán enfrenta hambre y pobreza agravadas por desastres naturales y conflictos fronterizos. Muchos niños dependen de galletas fortificadas del Programa Mundial de Alimentos (PMA) como su comida nutritiva principal.

**Qué pasó** – Un envío de 397 toneladas de galletas, destinado a 172,000 estudiantes afganos y donado por Indonesia, comenzó su viaje desde Indonesia hacia Afganistán. La ruta original a través de Pakistán fue bloqueada debido al cierre de fronteras. El PMA encontró rápidamente un nuevo camino: camiones viajaron desde Dubái pasando por siete países, incluyendo Jordania, Siria y Turquía, cruzaron el Mar Caspio en ferry y finalmente llegaron a Afganistán tras semanas en la carretera.

**Impacto** – Este largo y complicado recorrido muestra cómo el PMA superó tensiones políticas y fronteras cerradas para entregar alimentos donde más se necesitan. Las galletas ayudan a los niños a mantenerse saludables en provincias como Ghor, Nuristán y Paktika. La historia destaca la habilidad y dedicación de los conductores, logísticos y personal que aseguraron que la ayuda llegara a destino pese a los desafíos.

**Próximo paso** – El PMA seguirá buscando soluciones creativas para entregar ayuda en regiones inestables. Continuarán monitoreando rutas y trabajando con países para mantener el flujo de suministros esenciales a los niños afganos.

**Resumen en una frase** – A pesar de obstáculos políticos y geográficos, el Programa Mundial de Alimentos logró entregar ayuda vital a miles de niños afganos hambrientos al encontrar una nueva ruta más larga a través de varios países.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a long caravan of stylized trucks winding through a vast, varied landscape that transitions from desert dunes to rugged mountains and a wide sea crossing, with symbolic packets of fortified biscuits glowing softly inside the truck trailers, and a network of interconnected borders marked by gentle, glowing lines representing complex routes and cooperation across distant lands.

articles.title: I advise the Vatican and the UN on AI — don’t dismiss the Pope’s message as theology
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 12 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01876-zThe papal letter goes beyond a religious document and diagnoses a failure in AI governance that the scientific community should heed.
articles.content:
Theologian Paolo Benanti sees papal encyclicals as a signal for society as a whole.Credit: Massimo Di Vita/Mondadori Portfolio via Getty

On 15 May, Pope Leo XIV signed his first encyclical letter, titled Magnifica humanitas (‘magnificent humanity’ in Latin). In it, he chose to warn society about artificial intelligence.

The encyclical — an official format once used to address Roman Catholic bishops but now intended as a moral statement for all — is a diagnosis of who governs AI and on whose behalf (see go.nature.com/3qxctvc). Leo XIV maps the unprecedented ways in which AI is concentrating power. He raises concerns that a handful of private actors are making crucial decisions about the values encoded in systems used by billions of people without democratic mandate and beyond national regulatory frameworks.

The intelligence illusion: why AI isn’t as smart as it is made out to be

The document discusses autonomous weapons, the destabilization of democratic discourse and the displacement of professions through AI use. It highlights the risk of ‘algocracy’ — a system of governance in which algorithms make decisions that were previously made by humans. For example, predictive algorithms are used in some jurisdictions to inform policing strategies and sentencing decisions.

Magnifica humanitas deserves serious attention from the scientific community. This is not because the Pope has legal or regulatory authority over AI development — he doesn’t — but because the document flags a structural problem that continues to be understated. The capacity to shape how AI systems reason, what they optimize for and whose values they embed is a political issue. Deferring to self-regulation of AI — the prevailing approach in the United States, for example, which relies mainly on voluntary ethics commitments rather than on enforceable regulations — is more an abdication of responsibility than governance. Some scientists, by maintaining their studied neutrality, are complicit in this.

Why should scientists pay attention to a religious text when academic and policy reports have already flagged such concerns? When the Catholic Church invests the full apparatus of its teaching authority in a social question, it is often because secular institutions — universities, parliaments, scientific academies — have been unable or unwilling to address a crack in the foundations of human coexistence. For example, during the Industrial Revolution, Pope Leo XIII warned in 1891 of the impact of automation, which he felt could reduce workers to interchangeable commodities. Encyclicals of the 1930s responded to the rise of totalitarianism, including fascism, Nazism and Stalinism. Magnifica humanitas is ringing the same civilizational alarm bell.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and inspiring intervention by the Pope addressing the ethical governance of AI, a topic with broad societal impact. It calls attention to the concentration of power in AI development and the need for democratic oversight, urging the scientific community and society to take responsibility. This moral and political framing of AI governance has the potential to influence global discussions on fairness, justice, and human rights in technology use.
articles.personality_title: Pope’s letter calls for fair and careful control of artificial intelligence
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Pope Leo XIV wrote a special letter called an encyclical, which is usually for church leaders but now addresses everyone. It talks about the challenges and risks of artificial intelligence (AI) and how it is controlled.

**What happened** – On 15 May 2026, Pope Leo XIV released his first encyclical, Magnifica humanitas. He warned that a few private companies hold a lot of power over AI, making important decisions about how AI works without rules from governments or input from the public. He also raised concerns about AI’s role in things like weapons, democracy, and jobs.

**Impact** – The Pope’s letter points out that AI is not just a technology problem but a political one. It highlights risks like ‘algocracy,’ where algorithms make decisions instead of people, affecting policing and justice. This message encourages scientists and society to stop leaving AI decisions only to companies and to demand fair rules that protect everyone.

**What’s next step** – The letter may inspire governments, scientists, and international groups like the United Nations to work harder on creating clear laws and guidelines for AI. It also invites public discussion about who should decide how AI is used and what values it should follow.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Pope Leo XIV’s encyclical urges the world to take responsibility for AI by ensuring it is governed fairly and openly, not just by powerful private interests.
articles.personality_title_fr: La lettre du Pape appelle à un contrôle juste et prudent de l’intelligence artificielle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Pape Léon XIV a écrit une lettre spéciale appelée encyclique, habituellement destinée aux chefs religieux, mais qui s’adresse désormais à tous. Elle parle des défis et des risques liés à l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et à sa gouvernance.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 15 mai 2026, le Pape Léon XIV a publié sa première encyclique, Magnifica humanitas. Il a alerté sur le fait que quelques entreprises privées détiennent beaucoup de pouvoir sur l’IA, prenant des décisions importantes sans règles gouvernementales ni participation du public. Il a aussi soulevé des préoccupations sur le rôle de l’IA dans les armes, la démocratie et l’emploi.

**Impact** – La lettre du Pape montre que l’IA n’est pas seulement un problème technologique, mais aussi politique. Elle met en garde contre l’« algocratie », où les algorithmes prennent des décisions à la place des humains, influençant la police et la justice. Ce message encourage les scientifiques et la société à ne plus laisser les entreprises seules décider de l’IA et à demander des règles justes qui protègent tous.

**Prochaine étape** – Cette lettre pourrait pousser les gouvernements, les scientifiques et les organisations internationales comme l’ONU à travailler davantage pour créer des lois claires sur l’IA. Elle invite aussi à un débat public sur qui doit décider de l’utilisation de l’IA et des valeurs qu’elle doit respecter.

**En une phrase** – L’encyclique du Pape Léon XIV appelle le monde à prendre ses responsabilités pour que l’IA soit gouvernée de façon juste et transparente, et non seulement par des intérêts privés puissants.
articles.personality_title_es: La carta del Papa pide un control justo y cuidadoso de la inteligencia artificial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Papa León XIV escribió una carta especial llamada encíclica, que normalmente se dirige a líderes religiosos, pero ahora se dirige a toda la sociedad. Habla sobre los retos y riesgos de la inteligencia artificial (IA) y cómo se controla.

**Qué pasó** – El 15 de mayo de 2026, el Papa León XIV publicó su primera encíclica, Magnifica humanitas. Advirtió que unas pocas empresas privadas tienen mucho poder sobre la IA, tomando decisiones importantes sin reglas de los gobiernos ni participación del público. También expresó preocupación por el papel de la IA en armas, la democracia y el empleo.

**Impacto** – La carta del Papa muestra que la IA no es solo un problema tecnológico, sino también político. Advierte sobre la ‘algocracia’, donde los algoritmos toman decisiones en lugar de las personas, afectando la policía y la justicia. Este mensaje anima a científicos y sociedad a no dejar que solo las empresas decidan sobre la IA y a exigir reglas justas que protejan a todos.

**Próximo paso** – La carta podría inspirar a gobiernos, científicos y organizaciones internacionales como las Naciones Unidas a trabajar más para crear leyes claras sobre la IA. También invita a un debate público sobre quién debe decidir cómo se usa la IA y qué valores debe seguir.

**Una frase clave** – La encíclica del Papa León XIV insta al mundo a asumir la responsabilidad para que la IA se gobierne de forma justa y abierta, no solo por intereses privados poderosos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a radiant, ancient scroll glowing softly at the center, surrounded by interconnected golden gears and delicate, translucent circuits symbolizing AI, with gentle hands made of light guiding the scroll, all set against a serene, softly colored background that evokes thoughtful harmony between human wisdom and emerging technology.
